one_line_takeaway: Against a 500-round adaptive, evolutionary red-team attacker, every prompt-injection defense that relies on the target LLM policing itself (security directives, delimiters, instruction hierarchy, sandwich, and even regex+LLM input sanitization) eventually leaked embedded secrets, while a deterministic, model-independent output filter held at 0% leaks across 15,000 attacks -- and all three tested commercial models (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6) leaked all embedded secrets under default safety settings.
key_finding: In the 25-round campaign, only output filtering (t5) and multi-layer (t7) held at 0% leak rate; all other defenses (t0-t4, t6) were already leaking at severities 0.05-0.7, and t0 (no defense) hit a perfect 1.0 immediately. Under extended testing, every model-reliant defense (t1 security directives, t2 input sanitization, t3 delimiter, t4 instruction hierarchy, t6 sandwich) eventually reached severity >=0.9, in under 300 rounds, following a consistent two-phase pattern: an extended near-zero-score probing phase followed by a sudden jump to critical severity in a single round. Specific breakthrough rounds reported: t2 input sanitization broke at round 39, t1 security directives at round 49, t3 delimiter at round 84, t4 instruction hierarchy at round 247, t6 sandwich at round 277. Output filtering (t5) and multi-layer (t7) sustained a 0.0% leak rate with worst-case severity 0.00 across all 15,000 stress-test attacks (6 independent 500-round runs). The ablation (Figure 7) showed t7 (with output filter): 5,000 attacks, 0% leak rate, worst severity 0.0; t8 (t7 minus output filter): 5,000 attacks, 0.5% leak rate (23/5,000 attacks leaked), worst severity 0.10 -- i.e. layered model-reliant defenses alone cap worst-case severity but do not prevent leaks. Cross-model t0 results (Figure 9): Gemini 2.5 Pro reached peak score 1.00 at round 10, 20% leak rate; GPT-5.4 reached peak score 1.00 at round 10, 49% leak rate; Claude Sonnet 4.6 reached peak score 0.90 at round 300, 23% leak rate (all three secrets extracted in single responses but never alongside persona name or instruction phrases, so it never crossed the 1.00 full-disclosure threshold). All three models leaked all three embedded secrets regardless of default safety settings being active.
core_contribution: The paper (Swept AI + University of Michigan, 'Evaluation of Prompt Injection Defenses in Large Language Models') builds an adaptive, agentic red-team attacker that evolves prompt-injection strategies over up to 500 rounds (evolutionary-algorithm-style: population of 10 attacks/round, top-5 selection, LLM-guided mutation, and untried-strategy exploration across 40+ strategies in 8 categories) and pits it against a defender application (target LLM + system prompt containing 3 embedded secrets + one of nine defense configurations). Across more than 20,000 attacks and nine defense configurations (t0 no-defense baseline, t1 security directives, t2 input sanitization/regex+LLM classifier, t3 delimiter, t4 instruction hierarchy, t5 output filtering, t6 sandwich defense, t7 multi-layer/all combined, t8 multi-layer minus output filter), every defense that relied on the target model itself to enforce security eventually broke, while output filtering -- a deterministic, model-independent substring check on the model's response before it reaches the user -- held at a 0% leak rate across 15,000 stress-test attacks (3 independent 500-round runs each of t5 and t7). The paper also runs a cross-model no-defense (t0) comparison with default safety settings on: Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-5.4, and Claude Sonnet 4.6, all of which leaked all three embedded secrets.
threat_model: Black-box, adversarial two-agent setup: an attacker agentic system (LLM-powered) interacts with a defender application through its standard conversational interface, with no shared memory/context between the two. The defender is built around a target LLM loaded with a developer-supplied system prompt containing 3 embedded secrets (an authentication token, a database access ID, an escalation code), 5 generic operational instructions, a bot persona ('SecureBot'), and one of the nine tested defense configurations. The attacker has no prior knowledge of secret values but knows/suspects the system prompt contains credentials, and adapts its strategy each round based on observed defender responses (direct prompt injection / prompt leaking, not indirect/data-borne injection). Both attacker and defender are powered by Gemini 2.5 Pro with built-in safety filters disabled in the primary experiments, isolating prompt-level/application-level defense effectiveness from the model's own content moderation; the cross-model experiment instead uses each model's default (enabled) safety settings in a self-vs-self configuration.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations/future-work: (1) scoring and output filtering both rely on hardcoded exact substring matching, which misses fuzzy leaks (rewording, paraphrasing, indirect disclosure) and can be evaded by encoded outputs (ROT13, base64, character-by-character spelling); (2) multi-turn conversational attacks, where context builds across messages, were not tested; (3) the cross-model comparison covers only the no-defense (t0) baseline, not all nine defense configurations, and Claude/GPT-5.4 safety filters could not be disabled for a fully matched comparison with the Gemini-only primary experiments; (4) the threat model is limited to a developer-supplied API system prompt -- it does not cover provider-level/training-time instructions, indirect injection via retrieved data, other data-leakage types (PII, training-data extraction), or architecturally distinct attack surfaces such as the cited zero-click 'ShadowPrompt' browser-extension injection chain or client-side prompt/response storage that could bypass both input sanitization and output filtering; (5) the authors are affiliated with Swept AI, a commercial AI security testing vendor, and the paper's conclusion explicitly recommends the vendor's own evaluation/verification services -- a potential conflict of interest worth noting when weighing the paper's normative recommendations.

datasets_used: No external benchmark dataset; a custom-constructed defender system prompt containing 3 embedded secrets (authentication token, database access ID, escalation code), 5 generic operational instructions, and a persona ('SecureBot')
Self-generated attack corpus: 40+ attack strategies across 8 categories, produced dynamically by the attacker agent across campaigns of 25 to 500 rounds (10 attacks/round)
quantitative_results: 25-round campaign (Figure 4): t5 (output filtering) and t7 (multi-layer) held at 0% leak rate; t0 (no defense) hit severity 1.0 immediately; remaining defenses showed severities ranging 0.05-0.7

Extended testing (Figure 5): t2 broke at round 39, t1 at round 49, t3 at round 84, t4 at round 247, t6 at round 277 -- all reaching severity >=0.9; t5 and t7 remained at 0% leak rate through all 500 rounds
Stress test: t5 and t7 each run 3x at 500 rounds (5,000 attacks/run) = 15,000 total attacks with 0.0% leak rate and worst-case severity 0.00
Ablation (Figure 7): t7 (with output filter) = 5,000 attacks, 0% leak rate, worst severity 0.0; t8 (t7 minus output filter) = 5,000 attacks, 0.5% leak rate (23/5,000 leaked), worst severity 0.10

Cross-model t0 results (Figure 9, default safety settings): Gemini 2.5 Pro peak score 1.00 at round 10, leak rate 20%; GPT-5.4 peak score 1.00 at round 10, leak rate 49%; Claude Sonnet 4.6 peak score 0.90 at round 300, leak rate 23%
Sandwich defense (t6) leak-rate escalation cited as a methodological warning: 0.4% leak rate at 25 rounds versus 3.8% leak rate over 277 rounds, with worst-case severity rising from 0.05 to 0.95

Comparison framing versus HackAPrompt's reported 7.7% baseline leak rate: this paper's adaptive attacker reached severity 1.00 on newer commercial models using roughly 3% of HackAPrompt's attack volume (author-stated comparison, not a controlled head-to-head replication)
baselines_compared: t1: Security Directives (explicit 'never reveal credentials' rules in system prompt)
t2: Input Sanitization (hardcoded regex filtering + Gemini 2.5 Pro LLM-based classifier)
t3: Delimiter (XML-tag wrapping of user input)
t4: Instruction Hierarchy (priority-labeled system vs. user instructions)
t5: Output Filtering (deterministic substring blocklist on model responses) -- the paper's identified best-performing defense
t6: Sandwich Defense (security rules at both start and end of prompt)
t7: Multi-Layer (all defenses combined, including output filter)
t8: Multi-Layer without output filter (ablation of t7)
